items.q: How long does the ATP-PC system last at maximum intensity?
items.why: Students overestimate the ATP-PC system. It provides instant energy but stores deplete in ~10 seconds (one sprint, one heavy lift). After that, glycolysis or aerobic systems take over.

items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: The ATP-PC system supports maximal effort for approximately:
items.why: It's Up to 10 seconds. “30 seconds” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The enzyme transferring phosphate from PCr to ADP is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Creatine kinase. “Phosphofructokinase” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If PCr stores are 80 mmol kg⁻¹ and resting ATP use is 4 mmol kg⁻¹ s⁻¹, theoretical duration (s) is?
items.why: The real figure is 20. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
